primary_accounting_issue: Eligibility of intragroup dividend receivables for foreign exchange risk hedge designation in consolidated financial statements

primary_authority.reason: IFRS 9 paragraph 6.3.5 restricts hedged items to parties external to the reporting entity, while paragraph 6.3.6 provides a specific exception for foreign currency risk of intragroup monetary items in consolidated statements.

supporting_authority.reason: IAS 21 addresses how intragroup monetary items create foreign exchange exposure in consolidated statements and the conditions under which such FX differences affect consolidated profit or loss.

IFRIC 16 clarifies that hedge accounting applies only to foreign exchange differences between functional currency of foreign operation and parent entity's functional currency, providing guidance on the scope of allowable hedged risks in net investment hedges.

peripheral_authority.reason: Recognition and measurement of gains and losses on financial assets is not directly relevant to the eligibility of designating intragroup dividend FX risk as a hedged item.
Disclosure requirements for subsidiaries and restrictions are topically related but do not govern the accounting treatment for hedge designation eligibility.

treatment_families.family: Hedge accounting designation unavailable - FX risk recognised in P&L as it arises
Hedge accounting designation permitted for intragroup monetary item FX risk

approaches.label: No hedge designation - foreign exchange risk recognised in profit or loss
approaches.rationale_for_inclusion: When the general restriction in IFRS 9 paragraph 6.3.5 applies (or the paragraph 6.3.6 exception conditions are not met), foreign exchange differences on intragroup dividend receivables are recognised in profit or loss in the period they arise under IAS 21 paragraph 28.

approaches.label: Hedge accounting for intragroup monetary item foreign exchange exposure
approaches.rationale_for_inclusion: Under the exception in IFRS 9 paragraph 6.3.6, when an intragroup monetary item (dividend receivable) creates FX exposure not fully eliminated on consolidation and that exposure affects consolidated profit or loss, the foreign currency risk may qualify as a hedged item in a documented hedge accounting relationship.
